name: Ashtamangala
shortDescription: The Ashtamangala are a sacred suite of Eight Auspicious Symbols endemic to a number of Dharmic Traditions such as Hinduism, Jainism, and Buddhism. These symbols represent good fortune, prosperity, and auspiciousness.
description: The Ashtamangala (Eight Auspicious Symbols) are a group of symbols representing good fortune and auspiciousness in Buddhism. While the specific symbols and their interpretations can vary slightly across different Buddhist traditions (Tibetan, Chinese, etc.), the core set remains relatively consistent. These symbols are not merely decorative; they are deeply imbued with spiritual meaning and serve as reminders of the Buddha's teachings and the path to enlightenment. They are frequently used in rituals, ceremonies, art, and architecture to invoke blessings and create a positive environment. The symbols include: the Lotus Flower (symbolizing purity and enlightenment), the Conch Shell (representing the sound of the Dharma), the Endless Knot (symbolizing the interconnectedness of all things and the infinite wisdom of the Buddha), the Pair of Golden Fish (symbolizing happiness and freedom from suffering), the Victory Banner (representing the victory over ignorance and negative forces), the Dharma Wheel (representing the teachings of the Buddha and the cycle of Dharma), the Treasure Vase (symbolizing abundance and inexhaustible riches, both material and spiritual), and the Parasol (symbolizing protection from suffering and harmful influences). These symbols are often depicted together and are closely associated with the Buddha Shakyamuni and other important figures and concepts in Buddhism. The Ashtamangala are also often linked to the Four Noble Truths and the Noble Eightfold Path, serving as visual reminders of the core principles of Buddhist philosophy. The Dharma wheel specifically is a central symbol, and representations of the Ashtamangala are ubiquitous in Buddhist art and practice.

sources.title: The Handbook of Tibetan Buddhist Symbols
sources.author: Robert Beer

sources.title: Living Dharma: Teachings of Twelve Contemporary Masters
sources.author: Jack Kornfield
